claim_text: A suite of IQ metrics also has the benefit of capturing different types of degradations — Mason et al. found that no single IQM was best across all degradation types.

sub_claims.text: Mason 2020 found that no single IQM was best across all degradation types.

evidence.snippet: When dividing the data by degradation type, the variation in SROCC between IQMs is less clear (Fig. 4). This lack of variation is shown by the small number of statistically different performance of the IQMs (Table VI). NQM appears to perform particularly well for images degraded by noise as it has a statistically better performance than all other IQMs except FSIM for these images. IWSSIM performed poorly for images degraded by undersampling artifacts, showing statistically larger residuals compared to all other IQMs for these images.

FSIM and NQM also consistently demonstrated high correlations with the radiologist scores. Indeed, NQM had performed statistically better than VIF for images degraded with Gaussian noise, undersampling, or wavelet compression.

when the images are divided by degradation type, each IQM has a similar SROCC with the radiologists score. It is only when the different degradations are grouped together that the differences in performance of IQMs arise.

The VIF, FSIM, and NQM demonstrated the highest correlation with radiologists' opinions of MR image quality. However, these metrics come at the cost of longer calculation times, which may influence their use in future research. Differences in the performance of the IQMs was also largely lost when images are divided by degradation type.
there will always be variability in the subjective scoring and the IQMs and radiologists may have different sensitivities or preferences for different forms of degradation.

sub_claims.nuance: Mason does name VIF (and FSIM, NQM) as highest-correlating metrics overall, but within specific degradation types the best-performing IQM shifts (e.g., NQM beats VIF on Gaussian noise, undersampling, and wavelet compression; IWSSIM is worst on undersampling). The manuscript's paraphrase that 'no single IQM was best across all degradation types' is directly supported by these per-degradation reversals.

phrasings_tried: no single IQM best across all degradation types
different IQMs perform best for different degradation types (e.g., NQM vs VIF on noise/undersampling/wavelet compression)
best metric depends on degradation type — no universal winner
variation in IQM performance when data divided by degradation type
one metric does not dominate across Gaussian noise, blur, motion, Rician noise, undersampling, wavelet compression
attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Mason et al. present this as their own empirical finding from their 414-image, 10-IQM comparison; not credited to another primary source.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Manuscript frames Mason as motivation for using a suite of IQMs that capture different degradation types. Mason's per-degradation-type result (no single IQM dominant; NQM/VIF/FSIM trade wins across Gaussian noise, undersampling, wavelet compression) directly supports this framing. Context matches.
